title: Double Dog Crate Console
summary: A 6-foot console table that's two large dog crates — 2x2/2x4 frames with real rebar bars, gate-latched doors, and a planked 1x10 top. Furniture the wire crate never was.
description:
Wire dog crates work fine and look like jail. This build replaces two of them with one piece of actual furniture: a 76" console with two large crate bays, vertical 3/8" rebar bars, 2x2-framed doors on gate latches, and a stained 1x10 plank top that reads as a sofa table. The dogs get den-like enclosed comfort; the living room gets its dignity back.

The rebar is the honest engineering choice here. Wood dowels look similar and last exactly until a determined dog decides otherwise; steel rebar is chew-proof, cheap, and sold at every lumberyard. The installation trick makes it practical: drill 3/8" holes all the way THROUGH the top rail but only 1/2" DEEP into the bottom rail — after the frame is painted, each bar threads down through the top hole and drops into its bottom socket. No bar fights you, and painting happens without taping forty steel rods (the source learned that the hard way and says so).

Bar spacing is laid out from center: mark each opening's center, then 4" off center each way, and repeat. Keep the resulting gaps around 4" or less for a large dog — and mind your actual dog: a gap a muzzle can enter but not exit is the one measurement this project cannot get wrong.

Build the doors from the straightest 2x2s in your stack — the source flags doors as the part most likely to warp, and a warped door on a gate latch either won't latch or won't stop rattling. Doors get their rebar placed DURING assembly (glued frame around the bars), sized to the opening minus 1/4" gap all around, hung on narrow-profile hinges with latches mounted on the 2x2 framing.

One welfare note the plan takes seriously and so should you: latches keep dogs in; make sure adults can open them fast. Two gate latches, mounted where hands (not paws) reach them.
timeLabel: 10–16 hrs over a weekend or two

tools.note: 2-1/2" pocket screws build the frames. Practice joints on scrap 2x2 first — the source says so and 2x2 pocket holes deserve the warm-up.
The 3/8" bit matched to your rebar — through the top rail, 1/2" deep in the bottom.
The 1/4" plywood back — and it can cut rebar with a metal blade if you don't have a cutter.
Doubly so when cutting rebar — hot steel chips.

materials.name: 2x4, 10 ft
materials.note: Light, straight-grained boards are easier to pocket-hole cleanly.

materials.name: 2x2, 8 ft
materials.note: Frames and doors. Save the four straightest for the doors — they warp-proof the latches.

materials.name: Plywood, 1/4", 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Back panel. Underlayment or paneling works.

materials.name: Rebar, 3/8", 60 linear ft
materials.note: The bars. Have the yard cut it in half for transport.

materials.name: Gate latches
materials.note: Base 1-1/2" wide to land on the 2x2 framing. Mount where hands reach and paws don't.

materials.name: Paint or stain
materials.note: Frame painted BEFORE the rebar goes in. The source stained the top over an aged-wood accelerator.

cutList.note: Confirm against YOUR openings — doors are built to opening minus 1/4" gap all around.

steps.title: Build the front and back frames
steps.body:
Build two identical long frames — 2x2 and 2x4 rails between the legs — with 2-1/2" pocket screws and glue. Run a few practice pocket-hole joints on 2x2 scrap first; a 2x2 leaves no margin for a mis-set jig, and the practice pieces cost nothing.

Square both frames by diagonals; every later piece assumes they agree.

steps.title: Add the front dividers
steps.body: Install the cross members that split the FRONT frame into the two door openings and the center. These dividers carry the hinges and latches later — plumb and square here is door-fit there.

steps.title: Join the frames into the carcass
steps.body: Connect the front and back frames with the remaining cross members — pocket screws and glue. You now have a 6-foot two-bay skeleton that should sit dead flat; shim and adjust before the glue grabs if it doesn't.

steps.title: Back panel
steps.body: Cut the 1/4" plywood back with a circular saw and a straightedge, and attach with 3/4" brads or staples into every frame member it crosses. The back is the crate's fourth wall AND the carcass's anti-rack panel — nail it off properly.

steps.title: Drill for rebar — through the top, 1/2" into the bottom
steps.body:
Lay out the bars: mark each opening's center, then step 4" off center each way and repeat across the opening. Drill 3/8" holes clean THROUGH the top rail at each mark, and matching holes only 1/2" DEEP in the bottom rail below.

This asymmetry is the whole trick: after painting, each bar threads down through the top and seats in its bottom socket. Check the resulting gaps against your actual dog — a muzzle-width gap is the one dimension this build must not get wrong.

PAINT THE FRAME NOW, before any steel goes in.

steps.title: Build and finish the top
steps.body:
Edge-join the three 1x10s with 3/4" pocket holes, 1-1/4" screws, and glue. Sand hard with 120 grit, with the grain — this is the show surface. Stain (the source layered golden oak over an aged-wood accelerator after the accelerator alone read purple — test on scrap first).

Attach the top with the 1-3/4" self-tapping screws from below, watching that overhangs stay even as you go — the top squares up the whole console as it fastens.

steps.title: Build the doors around their bars
steps.body:
Cut and dry-fit each door frame to its opening minus 1/4" gap all around, from your four straightest 2x2s. Drill the bar holes, paint the frame parts (tape the bar ends), then glue the frame together WITH the rebar captured inside — door bars can't thread in later; the frame closes around them.

Clamp square and let the glue cure fully. A door is the highest-warp, highest-consequence part on the build; give it no excuses.

steps.title: Hang, latch, thread, done
steps.body:
Hang each door with even gaps on the narrow-profile hinges — pre-drill every screw hole; 2x2 stock near an edge splits on sight. Mount the gate latches on the 2x2 framing where adult hands reach easily.

Thread the frame bars down through the top holes into their sockets. Drop in the dog beds, latch the doors, and watch the dogs claim the den in under a minute. The wire crates go to the curb.

images.alt: Long console table with two built-in dog crate bays with rebar bars and doors
